attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with progressive weakness and numbness of her left foot over three days. She has a history of adult-onset asthma diagnosed two years ago, which has been difficult to control despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids. She also reports a three-month history of nasal congestion and crusting. Her only regular medication is fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 92/min, blood pressure 145/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is weakness of left foot dorsiflexion and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the left foot. Scattered wheeze is heard on chest auscultation. Blood tests reveal an eosinophil count of 2.5 × 10⁹/L (0.0-0.5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of features suggestive of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly known as Churg-Strauss syndrome): asthma, peripheral eosinophilia, and evidence of vasculitis (in this case, mononeuritis multiplex affecting the common peroneal nerve). The presence of upper airway symptoms (nasal congestion and crusting) further supports this diagnosis. Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis typically develops in three phases: allergic rhinitis/asthma, peripheral eosinophilia, and finally vasculitic manifestations. Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) are positive in approximately 40% of cases, usually with anti-myeloperoxidase (MPO) specificity.

Multiple sclerosis can cause neurological symptoms but typically presents with central rather than peripheral nervous system involvement. The combination with asthma and marked eosinophilia would be unusual.

Guillain-Barré syndrome typically presents with ascending symmetric weakness starting in the lower limbs. The presence of asthma, eosinophilia, and asymmetric neurological findings makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy usually presents with symmetric proximal and distal weakness and sensory symptoms. The associated features of asthma and eosinophilia would not fit with this diagnosis.

Common peroneal nerve entrapment can cause foot drop but would not explain the systemic symptoms, eosinophilia, or history of difficult-to-control asthma. Additionally, entrapment typically occurs at the fibular head and would not cause lateral foot sensory changes.

The diagnosis of EGPA is supported by the American College of Rheumatology criteria, which include asthma, eosinophilia >10%, neuropathy, pulmonary infiltrates, paranasal sinus abnormalities, and extravascular eosinophils on biopsy. Treatment typically involves systemic corticosteroids, with additional immunosuppression in severe cases.
learningPoints: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (formerly Churg-Strauss syndrome) typically presents with asthma, peripheral eosinophilia, and vasculitic manifestations.
The presence of mononeuritis multiplex strongly suggests a systemic vasculitis.
Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies are positive in about 40% of cases of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, usually with perinuclear pattern.
references: Emmi G, Bettiol A, Gelain E, Bajema IM, Berti A, Burns S, Cid MC, Cohen Tervaert JW, Cottin V, Durante E, Holle JU. Evidence-Based Guideline for the diagnosis and management of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Nature Reviews Rheumatology. 2023 Jun;19(6):378-93. Available at: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41584-023-00958-w
